prose: The pitted, industrial zinc-alloy of the bulkhead bit into {given_name}'s palm with a flat, sub-zero sting—the mere three millimeters of cast iron separating their fragile breath from the absolute silence of the outer dark. Beneath that skin-freezing metal lay the dry, rhythmic shudder of the air scrubbers, a relentless vibration that climbed the bones of the wrist, rattled the collarbone, and settled as a dull ache in the roots of the teeth. Everything in this high-frontier hold felt greasy and provisional, coated in the humid film of five hundred people breathing the same thin, twice-washed atmosphere. To lean against any structural pillar was to feel the collective shudder of a people huddled on a dry mountain of scrap, where the slightest structural groan of the plating felt like a finger tracing the spine.

In the center of the assembly room, the air grew thick with the salt-stink of wool coats and old grease, sticking to the back of the neck and pooling in the small of {given_name}'s back. The mine-bosses sat with their fingers splayed wide on the table—hands that had been scarred into calloused hide by decades of pneumatic grit and the stinging bite of high-pressure grease. They brought those heavy palms down against the sheet-metal table, the vibration humming through the floorboards and into the soles of {given_name}'s boots as they demanded the unfeeling precision of automated scrapers. Opposite them, the weavers and draymen of the lower steerage clutched their threadbare peaked caps with white-knuckled fingers, their palms so slick with sweat that the coarse wool grew dark and limp under their thumbs, their throats raspy from forty years of inhaling dry nitrogen and old dust.

The tension of the room pressed against {given_name}'s chest like a physical hand, a constriction that made the recycled draft feel as thin and flat as dry cardboard. It was the phantom pressure of an old inheritance, the same heavy ache that had stooped the shoulders of {family_name} weavers in the freezing, waterlogged basements of the old country, where the value of a man's sweat was weighed against the iron of the loom that bought him. Now, out on the freezing rim of the wilderness, where every lungful of oxygen was measured on a clerk's ledger, the choice between the preservation of human bread and the cold, mechanical efficiency of the scrubbers drew tight around {given_name}'s throat like a hempen cord.

As the voices rise to a sharp, dry roar, {given_name} feels the greasy draft of the recirculator lick the nape of their neck, their gaze drawn to a child in the back row who is desperately kneading a scrap of moth-eaten Tallis-wool—the small knuckles white and raw, seeking the familiar warmth of a home that now lies five light-years deep in the dust.

prose: The life-support rig does not sing; it wheezes with the dry, rattling phlegm of an old tailor’s chest, a low-oxygen stutter that has become the rhythmic background to every scrap of law they try to argue into existence here in this high, cold dark. Through the thin-gauge bulkheads of the orbital assembly, the noise of their survival is cheap and scraping—the frantic, tinny rattle of greywater rushing through salvaged copper pipes, the rhythmic, coughing click of carbon-scrubbers fighting the coal-black dust of four thousand tenements hung in the void. Beneath the shouting of the ward delegates and the low, muttered psalms of the elders, there is the raw, unceasing percussion of their poverty: the heavy, bone-shaking thump of manual sheet-presses and the high, iron scream of drills biting into low-grade scrap. It is the music of a congregation that has built its house of study out of the discard-pile of the stars, always one blown seal away from the great, silent Sabbath of the deep.

A sudden, tearing shriek of static rips through the shortwave receiver, a sound like frozen linen being ripped down the middle, freezing every voice in the gallery mid-syllable. Over the crackle of the long-range band comes the surveyor’s voice—thin, parched, dry as dust—reporting an uncatalogued drift-rock tumbling toward the central collector rigs, its mass whistling through the low-frequency radar-pings like a dropped coin rattling down a dark well. For three agonizing heartbeats, the hum of the entire habitat seems to dip, a collective intake of breath where the only sound is the wet, desperate hiss of oxygen regulators trying to match the panic of four thousand pairs of lungs. When the signal finally dies, it leaves behind a hollow, ringing quiet, a silence so dense and heavy it presses against the eardrums like the weight of the old ocean, reminding them how cheaply their leases on the sky have been bought.

Before the vibration of the near-miss can fade from the floorboards, the speakers pop and rattle again, drowned out by the clear, high-gain boom of the consortium's deep-space relays. Their voices are clean, loud, and utterly free of the static that plagues the steerage decks—a smooth, predatory purr of well-oiled gears and cold turbines that promises the stars to anyone willing to strip them of their soul. This high-altitude bluster, broadcast from the cushioned bulkheads of the directors' ships, grates like iron filings against the low, rhythmic chanting rising from the {family_name} cabins, where families huddle to whisper the prayers for the dead and the living alike, refusing to let the roar of the machine drown out the quiet language of their fathers. In the collision of these two signals—one a gilded trumpet of progress, the other a raspy, collective murmur of a people who refuse to vanish—the law of the wilderness is being hammered out, one deafening frequency at a time.

The telegraph-ribbon of the mining report clatters onto the table like dry bones, its inked numbers spelling out the cost of every ton of iron in lives and broken bulkheads, even as the rival line's high-frequency signals grow louder, crowding our bands with the arrogant noise of their expansion.

thread.crossing: The hot, resinous steam of cardamom tea rises from the Baghdadi trade factor's brass thermos, cutting through the thin, copper-tasting chill of our assembly bay as we negotiate under the dry, rhythmic rattle of the failing scrubbers. To save the tenement families from suffocating in this high wilderness, {given_name} {surname} signs over our hard-won orbital transit rights in exchange for their high-pressure oxygen seals. It is a cold, calculated bargain, binding our legal future to their ancient mercantile network just to keep the pumps turning.

prose: The phosphor-blue wireframes of the habitat extension hang suspended in the command deck’s damp gloom, casting cold, striped shadows across {given_name}’s brow like the weave of an ancient prayer shawl. It is a dizzying lattice of light—a miniature spine of glass and structural alloy designed to shelter ten thousand souls, its virtual hydroponic rings spinning like pale green coins beside the gray, skeletal knuckles of the drydock. To look into this glowing ribcage of the future is to see the long, circuitous wandering of the {family_name} line distilled into a single, fragile architectural draft: a wooden chest of a home rebuilt in steel against the absolute, dry-mouthed dark of the void. Every neon tracer, every twitching spark on the terminal, glows with the stubborn weight of old prayers whispered in crowded steerage, now writ vast across the high frontier.

Past the pulsing cage of the hologram, the wide, double-thick pane of the viewport holds the ancient cradle in its dark frame. There hangs the old world, a bruised, oil-slicked marble of indigo and soot, its gray cloud-banks twisting in slow, indifferent wreaths over the valleys where their grandfathers once packed their single satchels and fled the mud. It looks so small from this high perch—an old, salt-bitten basin of wars and forgotten leases—while the endless black ahead is pinpricked with the hard, unblinking glare of unmined rocks. The descendants of {surname} had once been nothing but dust on the margins of those distant highways; now, their gaze is fixed on the raw, uncarved dark of the outer belt, where the stars do not offer a soft twinkle but burn with the steady, white-hot intensity of sanctuary lamps.

Two blueprint projections hover side by side in the darkness, their differences cast in sharp, blue-and-amber light. The first draft is a severe, utilitarian column of iron-gray blocks and unadorned shafts, its lines as sharp and unforgiving as a ledger book—a machine designed only to crack the belt and feed the smelters without wasting a single breath of oxygen on beauty. The second draft is an interlocking hive of curved glass commons and wide, shared plazas, its sweeping arches recalling the crowded, bustling courtyards of the old quarters where the stove was always lit and neighbors spoke through open windows. This is no mere debate over load-bearing struts; it is a visible choice between a barracks of pure survival and a home where the memory of their people might actually take root in the dry soil of the stars.

The chief architect, their spectacles catching the cold neon glow of the console, points a gloved hand toward the severe, efficient lines of the first blueprint, waiting in tense silence for the word to build.

choice.text: Lean into the pragmatic vision: a habitat optimized for rapid resource extraction and minimal luxury.

prose: The chief architect, their face shadowed by the warm orange arc of the communal design, taps the glass terminal near the central plaza, waiting for a nod that will invite the old warmth into the dark.

options.text: Advocate for an open-door policy, welcoming all willing pioneers to the new worlds, regardless of background or initial wealth.

prose: The tongue, pickled by years in the steerage of the transit-berths, parsed the cold grease of the nutrient paste not as nourishment, but as a calcified, ancient patience. It was a flat, zinc-heavy smear against the throat, a chemical mimic of iron filings and soy engineered to sustain a pulse through three hundred standard days of lightless drift. To {given_name}, each tasteless swallow carried the stale draft of tenement halls and Atlantic crossings three centuries gone—a dry, alkaline ash that spoke of a people accustomed to packing their lives into pine crates. Yet beneath the sterile starch of the ration, there lived a stubborn, spectral salt, like the sweat of ancestors who had cleared the stony soils of the Old World only to look up at the cold, unblinking stars.

Here at the high table of the new colony-dome, the feast was an act of translation. {given_name} tasted the pale, flaked meat of the hydroponic carp—raised in recirculated cisterns, its flesh lacking the fat, mud-richness of the old rivers, yet spiked with a sharp, medicinal horseradish that brought sudden, stinging tears to the eyes. It was a reconstruction of survival, the salt and vinegar of the diaspora reconstituted under the yellow glare of atmospheric scrubbers. With each bite, the history of their house was re-asserted: a bitter herb to remember the clay, a sweet compound of dried apples and synthetic honey to soften the brick-mortar of their labor. The flavor of their persistence settled heavy and warm in the throat, a reminder that even among the cold stars, the covenant was kept in the salt.

Across the low-lit board, the heir tore a heel of synthesized challah, its crust lacquered with an artificial egg-wash that smelled of warm yeast and scorched copper. The child chewed with the serious, unhurried cadence of youth born to the vacuum, oblivious to the decades of grit it had taken to secure this first true harvest of the dome-farms. To watch them swallow this bread was to taste the sweet, yeasty dust of a frontier finally broken—a complex vintage of wild ambition, the iron of new steel, and the soft, lingering honey of a long-delayed peace. The flavor of the future was still raw, still fermenting in the dark of the sector, but as the heir reached for the cup, the dry residue of their long journey seemed at last to yield to the clean, cold water of home.

The heir, who is {given_name}'s successor, steps forward once the plates are cleared, their lips still carrying the faint, sweet trace of the honeyed apples as they squint into the cold, blue luminescence of a datapad mapping the raw void of the outer rim.

choice.text: Inspire the heir with a sense of boundless possibility, pointing them toward the untapped potential of uncharted frontiers waiting beyond the dark.

decision.prompt: As the grease of the transit-berths clings to the back of your throat, the cabin pressure drops, signaling the final approach. The long, dry wandering of the {surname} line has reached its next docking bay. How will you anchor your blood to these cold, metallic systems?

options.text: We must wash the taste of zinc from our mouths with wine from a glass we shatter ourselves under the gantry. I will take a partner from the old Odessa-crater community, building a proper home in the lower berths where we will raise children who know both the ancient prayers and the orbital paths.

The cold metal of these berths has taken too many parents already. I will not seek a spouse in this barren void, but I will adopt the sister's orphans who survived the pressure-drop, raising them in the law to carry the {family_name} mantle without tying myself to a new bond.

The dry, alkaline ash on my tongue is the taste of our future state. I cannot bring children into these pressurized tin cans; my life belongs to the migrant Assembly. I will pour every breath into winning our political charter, letting the {surname} name dissolve into the history of the free ports.
